retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Nephritic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: As with nephrotic syndrome, nephritic syndromes can also be primary or secondary. Some of the most common causes of nephritic syndrome are post-infectious glomerulonephritis, IgA nephropathy, and lupus nephritis. Poststreptococcal nephritic syndrome arises 2 to 3 weeks after group A B-hemolytic nephritogenic streptococcal infection of the skin (impetigo) or pharynx. It usually occurs in children but can also occur in adults. No characteristic finding on H&E stain but granular IF pattern and subepithelial immune complex deposition (“humps”) on EM. [24] IgA is the most common nephropathy worldwide. It is characterized by the IgA immune complex deposition in the mesangium of glomeruli. It commonly presents as hematuria following mucosal infections, especially gastroenteritis. [24]

retrieved_docs.content: IgA Nephropathy: usually occurs after an upper respiratory tract or gastrointestinal infection, but it differs from PSGN in the shorter latency period it takes to appear after the episode of infection. It can also be described as synpharyngitic hematuria- hematuria and infection coincide.

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality during the acute phase of glomerulonephritis has been estimated at around 2 and 12 percent. It has been assumed that the renal changes found in the majority of patients who survived the initial acute attack would be qualitatively similar to those noted in the fatal cases, although little direct evidence existed to support this view. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Differential Diagnoses
retrieved_docs.content: Following are some important differentials to be considered while making the diagnosis of glomerulonephritis: Acute kidney injury Crescentic glomerulonephritis Diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis Focal segmental glomerulonephritis Glomerulonephritis associated with nonstreptococcal infection Goodpasture syndrome Lupus nephritis Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis The following renal syndromes frequently mimic the early stages of acute GN: Idiopathic hematuria Chronic GN with an acute exacerbation Anaphylactoid purpura with nephritis Familial nephritis

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: When nephritic syndrome is the main presentation in children, it is likely post-infectious. In adults, however, IgA nephropathy should be considered. When systemic vasculitis involves glomeruli, the cause in the younger age group is Henoch Schonlein purpura, while in adults, granulomatosis with polyangiitis should be suspected. Lupus nephritis is seen more commonly in young women (20 to 30 years). [46]

retrieved_docs.title: C3 Glomerulopathy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: C3 glomerulopathy (C3G) is a rare glomerular disease stemming from dysregulation of the complement system's alternative pathway, resulting in excessive C3 protein deposits in the kidney’s glomeruli. Characterized by dominant C3 deposits without or with minimal immunoglobulin deposition, C3G primarily includes 2 subtypes: C3 glomerulonephritis and dense deposit disease. These subtypes are differentiated based on electron microscopy findings, with C3 glomerulonephritis showing scattered electron-dense deposits and dense deposit disease exhibiting a linear pattern within the glomerular basement membrane. C3G manifests with nephritic and nephrotic features, such as proteinuria, hematuria, and kidney function decline. This disease is often challenging to diagnose, as it shares overlapping features with other glomerulopathies, such as post-infectious or immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis. Understanding the underlying mechanisms, particularly the genetic mutations and autoantibodies affecting complement regulation, is crucial to accurate diagnosis and targeted treatment.

retrieved_docs.content: Nephrotic syndrome: 2 4-hour urine protein excretion is more than 3.5 gram/day along with hypoalbuminemia, edema, hypogammaglobulinemia, and increased risk of thrombosis (due to loss of pro-coagulants).

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Primary glomerulonephritis presenting as the nephrotic syndrome in young patients is likely to be minimal change disease, while in adults, membranous variety is more likely. [45] In the secondary category, diabetes mellitus has to be ruled out.

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Prognosis -- Among the Nephrotic Spectrum Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Minimal change disease has a very good prognosis for all ages if there is a response to corticosteroid therapy. The primary morbidity is related to the adverse effects of the medications. [44] Approximately a third of patients with membranous nephropathy who have subnephrotic proteinuria respond to conservative management. Spontaneous remission has also been seen in cases of heavy proteinuria. However, in others with features of nephrotic syndrome, remission may take up to 6 months, provided adequate treatment is given. Appropriate treatment does slow the progression of HIV-associated nephropathy, but with progression into ESRD, a kidney transplant may be necessary. Amyloid light-chain (AL) amyloidosis takes 2 to 3 years for progression towards ESRD, while for amyloid A (AA) amyloidosis, remission can be achieved by identifying and managing the underlying disease. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease, a condition that manifests as renal dysfunction, and it is also a key component of pulmonary‑renal syndrome. This form of glomerular injury encompasses the entities “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” which are localized to the glomerulus and represent specific types of proliferative glomerulonephritis. These three entities are further mapped to post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, indicating that they share pathological features with glomerular inflammation that follows an infectious episode. Proliferative glomerulonephritis, the broader category that includes “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” also contains systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ) as a member, highlighting the autoimmune contribution to this pattern of kidney disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑basement membrane disease, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, and systemic lupus erythematosus are interconnected through shared glomerular pathology and renal functional impairment, underscoring the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 726
retrieved_docs.content: Nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis are glomerular disorders that manifest in the glomerulus and are included as members of متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ). These conditions are conceptually linked to التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري (proliferative glomerulonephritis), which is recognized as a subtype of nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis and is also a member of الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ), reflecting its role in systemic lupus erythematosus–related kidney involvement. Furthermore, nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis are mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis), indicating a correspondence between these renal pathologies and the immune‑mediated response that follows certain infections. Together, these relationships highlight how various forms of glomerular inflammation, sclerosis, and nephrotic processes intersect within drug‑induced syndromes, autoimmune disease clusters, and post‑infectious contexts, underscoring the importance of recognizing shared pathogenic pathways for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), representing a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular inflammation. This disease is closely associated with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), a clinical entity in which acute lung involvement—often pulmonary hemorrhage—occurs together with rapid deterioration of renal function. Understanding that necrotizing glomerulonephritis both belongs to the spectrum of glomerulonephritis and can manifest as part of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for timely diagnosis, comprehensive evaluation of both kidney and lung pathology, and the implementation of appropriate immunosuppressive and supportive therapies to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified as a form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) and is frequently encountered as a component of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a severe, necrotizing inflammation of the renal glomeruli, it represents a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular pathology. Its association with pulmonary‑renal syndrome highlights the clinical pattern in which simultaneous lung hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis occur, often due to small‑vessel vasculitis or autoimmune mechanisms. Recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis as both a distinct glomerular disease and a manifestation of pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for accurate diagnosis, prompt immunosuppressive treatment, and coordinated care of the intertwined renal and pulmonary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: The glomerulus serves as the primary anatomic site where nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis manifest. These three renal pathologies are classified as a form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري (proliferative glomerulonephritis) and are also mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis). Proliferative glomerulonephritis encompasses systemic autoimmune disease such as الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ), indicating that systemic lupus erythematosus can present with proliferative glomerular inflammation. Recognizing the glomerular location of these conditions and their classification under both proliferative and post‑infectious glomerulonephritis aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing renal disease, particularly when systemic lupus erythematosus is involved.

Necrotizing glomerulonephritis occupies a distinct, severe position within renal pathology as a form of glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى). It is inversely related to general glomerulonephritis, linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), and can manifest as pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئية كلوية). Hierarchically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis serves as a parent condition for nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis, which together constitute the primary lesion sites of the glomerulus. Within the broader spectrum of glomerular disease, uremia is a subcategory, and proliferative glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري) represents a specific type of the nephritis‑nephrosis‑renal‑sclerosis group. Proliferative glomerulonephritis includes systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ)) and post‑infectious glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي) as member conditions,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2943
retrieved_docs.content: Nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis are identified as constituent renal manifestations of متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ), the drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms syndrome. These same renal entities are also mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي, a form of post‑infectious glomerulonephritis. This overlap indicates that kidney involvement in the drug‑induced hypersensitivity syndrome may present as nephritis, nephrosis, or renal sclerosis, and that these pathologies share clinical and pathological features with post‑streptococcal glomerular disease. Recognizing this relationship assists clinicians in monitoring renal function, diagnosing renal complications of the drug reaction, and differentiating them from other post‑infectious renal disorders.

Acute glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد) is a broad clinical category that encompasses nephritis, nephrosis, nephrotic syndrome, and renal sclerosis; it is synonymous with acute kidney inflammation (التهاب الكلية الحاد) and is a member of acute renal failure (الفشل الكلوي الحاد (SMQ)). Morphological subtypes of acute glomerulitis include crescentic glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي) and hyperplastic glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري). Hyperplastic glomerulitis is also mapped to post‑inflammatory glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي) and can manifest in systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ)). Pediatric Nephrology Terminology adds متلازمة انحلال الدم الوريمي (hemolytic‑uremic syndrome) and crescentic glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي) as conditions linked to the CKD SMQ grouping; both are considered members of the chronic kidney disease framework. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 776
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Nephrology Terminology defines a hierarchy of renal disease concepts that includes متلازمة انحلال الدم الوريمي (Hemolytic‑Uremic Syndrome) and التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي (Hyaline glomerulonephritis). متلازمة انحلال الدم الوريمي is classified as a member of المرض الكلوي المزمن (SMQ) (Chronic Kidney Disease SMQ), and التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي is also linked to المرض الكلوي المزمن (SMQ), indicating that both conditions are associated with chronic kidney disease. The glomerulus is identified as the finding site of nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis, establishing the glomerulus as the primary anatomic location for these pathologic processes. Within the broader category of nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis, التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي is a child concept, representing a specific subtype of glomerular injury. Conversely, التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري (Proliferative glomerulonephritis) is related as an inverse subclass of the same broader category and is mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (Post‑infectious glomerulonephritis), highlighting a clinical correlation between proliferative and post‑infectious forms. Additionally, التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري includes الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ) (Systemic Lupus Erythematosus SMQ) as a member, linking this specific glomerular pathology to a systemic autoimmune disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how pediatric nephrology organizes individual glomerular diseases under larger nephritic and nephrotic groupings, connects them to key anatomical sites, and relates them to systemic conditions such as chronic kidney disease, Hemolytic‑Uremic Syndrome, and systemic lupus erythematosus, thereby supporting comprehensive classification and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) and is directly linked to متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). In the broader context, التهاب كبيبات الكلى is clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure), indicating that patients with this type of glomerular inflammation are at risk for progressive loss of kidney function. Recognizing the relationship between necrotizing glomerulonephritis, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and the potential development of chronic renal failure is essential for timely diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 738
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) that is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease). This association reflects the pathogenic role of anti‑GBM antibodies, which can produce both renal injury and the متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a manifestation of اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction), necrotizing glomerulonephritis often progresses to chronic kidney impairment, clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure). The disease spectrum encompassed by the collective term nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis includes a range of inflammatory and sclerotic renal disorders. These conditions share the glomerulus as their primary site of pathology (finding_site_of glomerulus) and are mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis), highlighting a common pathway of glomerular injury following immune‑mediated inflammation. Within this broader group, متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ) (drug‑induced hypersensitivity syndrome with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) is recognized as a member, underscoring the potential for systemic drug reactions to precipitate renal inflammation and sclerosis. Overall, necrotizing glomerulonephritis and its related entities illustrate the interconnected nature of immune‑mediated renal diseases, their systemic manifestations, and the importance of recognizing both primary glomerular pathology and secondary contributors such as drug‑induced syndromes for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1170
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific form of glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى) that is classified as a child condition under the broader categories of nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis. It is associated with an anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي) and commonly occurs in the setting of pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية). The glomerulus is identified as the primary site of pathology for nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis, and these entities are mapped to post‑streptococcal glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي). Within the hierarchy of glomerular disease, اعتلال كبيبات الكلى also has uremia (اليوريمية) as a downstream manifestation. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis fits into the spectrum of glomerular injuries, its immunologic associations, and its clinical sequelae, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1248
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified under the broader category of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerular disease). It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), reflecting an immunologic mechanism that targets the glomerular basement membrane. Clinically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often occurs within متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), linking severe renal inflammation with pulmonary hemorrhage. Understanding these interconnections is essential for accurate diagnosis and coordinated management of patients who present with combined kidney and lung involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 969
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, a pattern that is also observed in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and in chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). Necrotizing glomerulonephritis represents a specific, severe form of glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) and is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), an autoimmune condition that manifests as renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). In addition, necrotizing glomerulonephritis is frequently associated with pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية), reflecting its systemic impact. Glomerulonephritis, in turn, is clinically associated with the development of chronic kidney failure, underscoring the progressive nature of renal injury in these disorders. Together, these relationships highlight how chronic inflammatory processes, autoimmune kidney diseases, and metabolic complications interrelate and contribute to long‑term renal impairment, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.
